key_insight: High-pressure torsion nanostructures the TiZrHfNbTa refractory high-entropy alloy so strongly that phonon transport is suppressed much more than electron transport, causing thermal and electrical conductivity to evolve differently.
engineering_relevance: This shows how severe plastic deformation can tune heat and charge transport in ultra-high-temperature alloys for applications where thermal management and mechanical strength must be balanced.
method: Microstructural defect engineering: dislocations, nanograin refinement, partial BCC-to-omega transformation
Thermal/electrical transport analysis using the Wiedemann-Franz law
evidence_signal: The abstract reports ~40 nm average grain size, hardness saturation with processing, and a phonon contribution of only 11–23% to thermal conductivity depending on strain level.
watch_item: The transport trends are material- and processing-state dependent, so the reported balance between electron and phonon contributions may not generalize to other refractory HEAs or HPT conditions.
paper_title: Thermal and electrical conductivity of a refractory high-entropy alloy after high-pressure torsion: Electron versus phonon contributions
